one_line_takeaway: Making image watermarking robust to adversarial perturbations (via empirical adversarial training or the paper's own certified randomized-smoothing method) systematically increases a previously unstudied 'identity leakage' vulnerability that lets attackers forge or extract watermarks from residual-image information, and the paper's residual-information-loss term (LRIL) only partially closes this robustness-vs-identity-leakage trade-off.
key_finding: Robust training measurably worsens identity leakage relative to clean training. On the identity forgery attack at m=30 (Table 3), forged bit accuracy for StegaStamp on COCO rises from 66.54% (clean vanilla) to 98.99% (W-ER) and to 91.10-96.66% across Gaussian-noise sigmas for W-CR (81.34-96.66% under affine-noise variants); on CelebA it rises from 84.80% (clean) to 100.0% (W-ER) and 92.13-100.0%-range for W-CR variants. HiDDeN shows smaller but consistent increases (e.g., COCO HiDDeN: 54.46% clean to 55.93-58.97% robust variants). The identity extraction attack bit accuracy for W-ER and W-CR exceeds 90% on StegaStamp in several settings. The paper's mitigation, LRIL, reduces this leakage: e.g., average forged bit accuracy for W-CR (Gaussian) drops from 76.79% (w/o LRIL) to 69.49% (w/ LRIL), and for W-CR (Affine) from 74.57% to 67.67%; average identity-extraction attack bit accuracy for W-ER drops from 82.50% (w/o LRIL) to 72.50% (w/ LRIL), comparable to the clean model's 72.25% (w/o LRIL). For identity linking, W-CR under Gaussian noise achieves a silhouette score of 0.4181 with LRIL, described as second-highest identity protection among tested configurations. On certified robustness, W-CR achieves average certified accuracy of 99.57% under additive Gaussian noise and 98.77% under affine transformations across all datasets/models, while sacrificing only marginal bit accuracy and 'nearly no clean accuracy' relative to the clean model; certified accuracy decays with increasing certification radius and drops to 0 past a threshold, with larger training-noise magnitude enlarging the certifiable radius (standard randomized-smoothing behavior).
core_contribution: The paper identifies a new attack surface in deep-learning-based post-processing image watermarking called 'identity leakage': an adversary who never decodes a watermark can still forge watermarked images carrying a target user's genuine watermark message (identity forgery attack, using as few as m=1 or m=30 of the victim's watermarked images), or can bit-by-bit decipher a specific watermark message by repeatedly querying an embedding service (identity extraction attack), by exploiting the fact that the decoder network extracts the watermark signal not only from the watermarked image but also from the 'residual image' (watermarked image minus original). The paper shows empirically that training a watermarking encoder/decoder to be robust against adversarial perturbations (via empirical adversarial training, W-ER, or via a proposed certified-robustness method, W-CR based on randomized smoothing) makes this identity leakage substantially worse, because the added noise during training increases how much watermark information leaks into the residual. To fix this trade-off the authors propose W-IR, a watermarking framework that combines (1) a novel randomized-smoothing-based certified robustness construction covering both pixel-level (additive noise) and coordinate-level (affine) perturbation spaces, formulated by treating the decoder+verification pipeline as a classifier, and (2) a residual information loss (LRIL), derived via a variational-inference bound on mutual information, that is added to training to maximize identity information retained in the watermarked image while minimizing (an upper bound on) identity information leaked into the residual image. W-IR is designed to be pluggable onto both empirically robust (W-ER) and certified (W-CR) watermarking pipelines.
threat_model: Two watermarking pipelines are attacked, both invisible post-processing schemes: StegaStamp and HiDDeN, each optionally hardened with empirical robust training (+noise-layer augmentation, W-ER) or with the paper's certified robust training (W-CR, randomized smoothing against additive Gaussian noise or affine transforms). Attacker capability for identity forgery: possession of one (m=1) or thirty (m=30) images that are already watermarked with the target's message (no access to encoder/decoder weights, no decoding needed) plus estimation of the residual image via low-pass filtering or a VAE; the attacker crafts a new image whose residual mimics the target's, causing the watermark authentication step to falsely verify the forged image as bearing the target's watermark. Attacker capability for identity extraction: black-box query access to the watermark embedding service (can request the same image be watermarked repeatedly / observe outputs) to bit-by-bit recover the secret watermark message. Neither attack requires access to gradients of the decoder or the original watermark message; the practical harm cited is undermining ownership tracking / copyright verification and use cases like police evidence authentication, where a forged 'authenticated' image could be attributed to an innocent party or an attacker could impersonate a legitimate watermark holder.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) adversarial training with larger perturbation magnitudes failed to converge for HiDDeN/StegaStamp, a limitation attributed to those magnitudes not being part of the original training recipes (footnote 4) -- so the empirical robustness (W-ER) comparisons are bounded by achievable noise levels. (2) The residual image used by the attacker is only an estimate (via low-pass filtering or a VAE), not the ground-truth residual, meaning attack success is contingent on residual-estimation quality, though the paper reports this is already sufficient for high attack success rates. (3) LRIL mitigates but does not eliminate identity leakage -- reported attack numbers after applying LRIL remain substantial (e.g., W-CR affine forged bit accuracy still 67.67% on average versus 65.55% for the best clean-model baseline), so the paper explicitly frames this as a balance/trade-off rather than a full fix. (4) Evaluation is limited to two watermarking backbones (StegaStamp, HiDDeN) and two datasets (CelebA-HQ, MS-COCO) at a fixed low resolution (128x128), so generalization to other watermarking architectures, larger images, or other robustness-training techniques (e.g., diffusion-based purification) is not directly tested in the reviewed sections. (5) Mutual information is not explicitly estimated; the LRIL loss is derived as a variational surrogate/upper-bound proxy, which the authors acknowledge is 'a well-known challenging problem' -- exactness of the bound is not empirically verified against a ground-truth MI estimate.

quantitative_results: Identity forgery (m=30), forged bit accuracy, COCO+StegaStamp: clean vanilla 66.54% -> W-ER 98.99% -> W-CR (Gaussian sigma 0.10/0.25/0.50) 91.10%/95.22%/96.66% -> W-CR (Affine sigma 0.01/0.02/0.03) 81.34%/77.97%/81.79%
Identity forgery (m=30), forged bit accuracy, CelebA+StegaStamp: clean vanilla 84.80% -> W-ER 100.0% -> W-CR (Gaussian) 98.61%/98.74%/92.13% -> W-CR (Affine) 94.29%/95.39%/91.56%
Average forged bit accuracy (Table 7, identity forgery): clean vanilla 65.55% (w/o LRIL) / 59.92% (w/ LRIL); W-ER 78.43%/75.93%; W-CR Gaussian 76.79%/69.49%; W-CR Affine 74.57%/67.67%
Average attack bit accuracy (Table 7, identity extraction): clean vanilla 72.25%/68.75%; W-ER 82.50%/72.50%; W-CR Gaussian 81.75%/75.50%; W-CR Affine 78.50%/73.50%
Certified accuracy of W-CR: average 99.57% under additive Gaussian noise and 98.77% under affine transformations across all datasets/models
Identity linking silhouette score for W-CR under Gaussian noise with LRIL: 0.4181 (second-highest identity protection among tested configurations)
Certification protocol: 500 test samples/dataset, N0=100, N=10^5, alpha=0.001
baselines_compared: Clean vanilla watermarking model (no robustness training)
Empirically robust W-ER (adversarial/noise-layer training per original StegaStamp and HiDDeN recipes)
Crypto-based watermark scheme (t = func(HMAC(key, original image))) [20]
W-CR without residual information loss (LRIL) as an ablation baseline
